children.name: Lycosa tarantula
children.description: The original tarantula -- name derives from Taranto in southern Italy. Historically blamed for tarantism. The New World theraphosids later borrowed the name.

children.name: Lycosa praegrandis
children.description: One of the largest European wolf spiders, found from the Balkans to the Middle East; digs deep silk-lined burrows.

children.name: Lycosa singoriensis
children.description: A large burrowing wolf spider of the steppes from Eastern Europe to Central Asia; its bite is medically minor.

children.name: Lycosa erythrognatha
children.description: Common urban wolf spider of southern Brazil; historically one of the most frequent causes of spider bites in the region before antivenom studies cleared it of serious danger.

children.name: Lycosa hispanica
children.description: A large burrowing wolf spider of the Iberian Peninsula. Builds turreted burrows in sandy soils and is one of the largest wolf spiders in Europe.

children.name: Lycosa fasciapilosa
children.description: A large striped wolf spider of Central Asia and Siberia. The specific name refers to the tufts of hair on its robust legs.

children.name: Hogna carolinensis
children.description: Largest wolf spider in North America; body up to 35mm. Female carries egg sac on spinnerets; spiderlings ride on her back.

children.name: Hogna radiata
children.description: Widespread across southern Europe, North Africa and the Middle East; recognised by a radiating pattern on the carapace.

children.name: Hogna ingens
children.description: Critically Endangered giant wolf spider restricted to a single valley on Deserta Grande in Madeira; one of the world's rarest spiders.

children.name: Hogna helluo
children.description: A fast-running North American wolf spider with a dark stripe down the centre of the carapace. Common in fields and gardens.

children.name: Hogna lenta
children.description: A medium-sized hogna of the southeastern US, found in woodland leaf litter. Despite the name, it is capable of quick bursts of speed when hunting.

children.name: Hogna pseudoradiata
children.description: A Central Asian hogna similar to H. radiata but with a less distinct carapace pattern. Digs burrows in arid steppe habitats.

children.name: Hogna baltimoriana
children.description: A North American wolf spider first described from the Baltimore area. Distinguished by a row of white spots along the dorsal midline of the abdomen.

children.name: Pardosa amentata
children.description: Extremely abundant in Sweden; on shores of lakes, bogs, and wet meadows. Females carry egg sacs and guard spiderlings. Males perform leg-waving courtship.

children.name: Pardosa lugubris
children.description: Common woodland wolf spider across Europe including Sweden; active on forest floor in spring and early summer.

children.name: Pardosa palustris
children.description: Abundant in open grassland and dunes across the Palearctic; an important predator of small insects in agricultural fields.

children.name: Pardosa monticola
children.description: Found in short dry grassland and heath; one of the commonest wolf spiders of European calcareous habitats.

children.name: Pardosa pullata
children.description: A very common small dark wolf spider of damp meadows and bogs throughout northern Europe.

children.name: Pardosa prativaga
children.description: A very common Pardosa of damp grasslands across Europe and Britain. Males have dark, hairy legs and perform distinctive visual courtship displays.

children.name: Pardosa agrestis
children.description: A Palearctic wolf spider of open fields and agricultural land. Adapted to disturbed habitats and an important natural pest controller in cereal crops.

children.name: Pardosa hortensis
children.description: A Mediterranean wolf spider that extends across southern Europe and into Central Asia. Often found in gardens and vineyards.

children.name: Pardosa saltans
children.description: A jumping wolf spider species, formerly part of the lugubris complex. Males perform elaborate visual courtship dances with raised legs.

children.name: Pardosa riparia
children.description: A small dark wolf spider of riverbanks and gravelly lakeshores across Europe. Well-camouflaged against the pebbly substrate.

children.name: Pardosa nigriceps
children.description: A striking sexually-dimorphic wolf spider: males have black head regions and brightly banded legs used in courtship displays. Common in European grasslands.

children.name: Pardosa hyperborea
children.description: A Holarctic wolf spider of the Arctic tundra. Active for only a few weeks during the brief Arctic summer. A key tundra predator of springtails and small flies.

children.name: Pardosa groenlandica
children.description: The only wolf spider of Greenland, also found in Iceland and Arctic Canada. Inhabits fell-fields and tundra; completes its life cycle over two Arctic summers.

children.name: Pirata piraticus
children.description: Semiaquatic; capable of walking on water. Common in Swedish bogs and lakeshores. Named pirate for its ability to sail on water surfaces.

children.name: Pirata hygrophilus
children.description: A semiaquatic wolf spider of damp woodland and marsh margins across Europe and northern Asia.

children.name: Pirata piscatorius
children.description: A large semiaquatic Pirata that can run across the water surface. Often found on peat bogs and the margins of ponds and slow streams.

children.name: Pirata tenuitarsis
children.description: A Palearctic semiaquatic wolf spider of Sphagnum bogs and wet moorland. The species name refers to its slender tarsi.

children.name: Pirata sedentarius
children.description: A North American pirate spider that sits at the entrance of its silk-lined burrow near water, darting out to capture passing insects.

children.name: Arctosa cinerea
children.commonName: Great raft spider relative / River wolf spider
children.description: The largest European Arctosa; lives in burrows in sand and gravel along river banks and lake shores; well camouflaged against substrate.

children.name: Arctosa perita
children.description: A coastal and heathland specialist of sandy ground; its mottled colouration matches the sand grains it lives among.

children.name: Arctosa leopardus
children.description: A striking spotted riparian wolf spider with leopard-like markings on the abdomen. Found on the edges of bogs, lakes and slow rivers across Europe.

children.name: Arctosa luteitana
children.description: A rare riparian wolf spider of western France and the Iberian Peninsula. One of the largest Arctosa species; restricted to sparsely vegetated sand and gravel bars along rivers.

children.name: Arctosa maculata
children.description: A small but beautifully marked wolf spider of Mediterranean island and coastal habitats, with a distinctly mottled black-on-cream pattern.

children.name: Arctosa alpigena
children.description: A high-altitude wolf spider of the Alps and other European mountain ranges, found above the treeline among rocks and alpine turf. Also occurs in the Arctic.

children.name: Arctosa virgo
children.description: A rare riparian wolf spider restricted to the Mediterranean islands of Corsica and Sardinia. Found on sand and gravel beds of intermittent streams.

children.name: Geolycosa missouriensis
children.description: Digs vertical burrows up to 30 cm deep in open sandy ground of central North America; ambushes prey from the burrow mouth.

children.name: Geolycosa wrighti
children.description: A burrowing wolf spider of the Great Lakes region, named after the naturalist John Henry Wright. Builds vertical burrows in sandy soils.

children.name: Geolycosa rafaelana
children.description: A burrowing wolf spider of the southwestern US, specialised in loose sandy soils. Spends nearly its entire life inside its vertical burrow.

children.name: Geolycosa micanopy
children.description: A Florida-endemic burrowing wolf spider, named after the historic town of Micanopy. Its burrow has a low turret of silk and sand.

children.name: Geolycosa pikei
children.description: A North American burrowing wolf spider of coastal sand dunes and sandy clearings. Named for the collector Pike.

children.name: Trochosa ruricola
children.description: A robust ground-dwelling wolf spider of damp grassland and field edges; widespread across the Holarctic, now also in North America.

children.name: Trochosa terricola
children.description: One of the most common woodland-floor wolf spiders in Europe; active mainly at night.

children.name: Trochosa spinipalpis
children.description: A Trochosa of damp woodland and fenland. Males have distinctive spines on the palpal tibia used in courtship. Widespread across northern Europe.

children.name: Trochosa hispanica
children.description: A Mediterranean Trochosa from Spain and Portugal. Dwells in damp leaf litter of riverine forests.

children.name: Trochosa hungarica
children.description: A central and eastern European Trochosa found in dry grasslands and steppes. Formerly treated as a subspecies of T. terricola.

children.name: Alopecosa fabrilis
children.description: A large, rare heathland wolf spider; rediscovered in Britain in 2020 after decades thought locally extinct.

children.name: Alopecosa cuneata
children.description: Males have distinctively swollen front legs used to clamp the female during courtship; common in dry grassland.

children.name: Alopecosa aculeata
children.description: A Holarctic wolf spider of northern coniferous forests and heaths.

children.name: Alopecosa pulverulenta
children.description: Possibly the most abundant wolf spider in Europe; found in almost every open habitat from gardens to mountains.

children.name: Alopecosa barbipes
children.description: A European fox-spider with long bristly leg hairs (beard) on the front legs of males. Found in dry grasslands and heathland.

children.name: Alopecosa trabalis
children.description: A large, handsome Alopecosa with two distinctive pale longitudinal stripes on the carapace. Active on sunny days in dry open woodland and heath.

children.name: Alopecosa farinosa
children.description: A Palearctic Alopecosa of dry, sandy habitats, with a mottled, powdery appearance that provides excellent camouflage on bare ground.

children.name: Alopecosa solitaria
children.description: A large, solitary Alopecosa of dry steppe and sandy habitats from eastern Europe to Central Asia. Builds a burrow lined with silk.

children.name: Alopecosa striatipes
children.description: A Eurasian fox-spider with conspicuously striped legs. Active in late summer in dry grassland habitats.

children.name: Alopecosa pinetorum
children.description: A northern European Alopecosa of pine forests and boreal woodlands. Active on the forest floor among pine needles and moss.

children.name: Rabidosa rabida
children.description: A conspicuous striped wolf spider of the eastern United States; the name refers to its fast, erratic running, not any disease.

children.name: Rabidosa punctulata
children.description: Recognised by a row of dark spots on the underside of the abdomen; common in tall grass of the eastern US.

children.name: Rabidosa hentzi
children.description: A North American rabid wolf spider, distinguished by darker longitudinal stripes than R. rabida. Common in grassy habitats across the southeastern US.

children.name: Schizocosa
children.description: A genus of medium-sized wolf spiders known for the striking courtship displays of males, who sport dark bristle brushes (ornamentation) on the forelegs used in visual and seismic signalling to females. Found across North America.

children.name: Schizocosa crassipalpis
children.description: A common North American Schizocosa, named for the male's swollen dark palps. Males have dense black bristles on the forelegs used in courtship displays.

children.name: Schizocosa ocreata
children.description: The best-studied Schizocosa. Males have black brushes on the fore tibiae and perform elaborate leg-waving courtship combining visual and vibratory signals. A model organism in spider behavioural evolution research.

children.name: Schizocosa rostrata
children.description: A North American Schizocosa with a distinctively projecting (beaked) carapace. Males lack the dense brushes of S. ocreata but still perform courtship displays.

children.name: Schizocosa avida
children.description: A large, aggressive wolf spider of the eastern and central US. Males have moderate leg brushes and use a combination of visual and seismic signals during courtship.

children.name: Schizocosa stridulans
children.description: A southeastern US Schizocosa that produces audible stridulatory sounds during courtship, in addition to visual displays.

children.name: Schizocosa ceratophila
children.description: A Schizocosa from the southeastern US with distinctive horn-like projections on the male palps. Closely related to S. stridulans.

children.name: Tigrosa
children.description: A recently elevated genus separated from Hogna, comprising large wolf spiders of wet habitats across North America. Females carry the egg sac on their spinnerets and the young ride on the mother's back after hatching.

children.name: Tigrosa aspersiventris
children.description: A large swamp wolf spider of the Great Lakes and eastern US, with speckled markings on the underside of the abdomen. Found in marshes and damp woodlands.

children.name: Tigrosa georgicola
children.description: A southeastern US swamp wolf spider from Georgia and surrounding states. Inhabits moist forest floors and swamps.

children.name: Tigrosa grandis
children.description: A large Tigrosa from the Gulf Coast of the United States. One of the largest lycosids in the region.

children.name: Gladicosa
children.description: A small North American genus of medium-sized wolf spiders with a distinctively rounded, bowl-shaped carapace. They are typically found in wooded habitats and use well-developed eyesight for hunting.

children.name: Gladicosa pulchra
children.description: A handsome wolf spider of the eastern US with a striking yellow and black carapace pattern. Found in leaf litter in deciduous forests.

children.name: Gladicosa bellua
children.description: A large Gladicosa from the southeastern US. The species name means 'beast' in Latin.

children.name: Gladicosa euchroma
children.description: A colourful eastern North American Gladicosa with a rich brown and orange colouration. Found in woodland leaf litter.

children.name: Sosippus
children.description: Unique among wolf spiders for building a large, funnel-shaped web with a silken sheet, somewhat resembling a grass spider web. Unlike most lycosids, which are wandering hunters, Sosippus waits at the funnel entrance for prey.

children.name: Sosippus mimus
children.description: A Florida and Gulf Coast web-building wolf spider that constructs a funnel-web sheet in low vegetation. Specimens were formerly misidentified as other funnel-web species.

children.name: Sosippus floridanus
children.description: A Florida-endemic Sosippus that builds a large funnel web in palmetto shrubs and grass tussocks. The female stays with the egg sac in a silk retreat.

children.name: Sosippus lindsayi
children.description: A southwestern US web-building wolf spider found in Arizona, New Mexico and Texas. Builds funnel-shaped webs in arroyos and rocky slopes.

children.name: Allocosa
children.description: A large and diverse genus of wolf spiders found on all continents except Antarctica. They are typically medium-sized, cryptically coloured, and prefer arid or sandy habitats. Many species build burrows in sand.

children.name: Allocosa senex
children.description: A South American Allocosa from Brazil and Argentina found in sandy coastal habitats. The species engages in remarkable biparental burrow care, behaviour almost unique among spiders.

children.name: Allocosa abscheinkleri
children.description: A Central Asian wandering wolf spider of dry steppes and semi-deserts. Digs burrows in sandy soil.

children.name: Allocosa brasiliensis
children.description: A South American Allocosa found from Peru to Argentina. Occurs in a wide variety of arid and semi-arid habitats.

children.name: Varacosa
children.description: A small North American genus separated from the Lycosa-Hogna complex. They are medium-sized wolf spiders found in open habitats including grasslands and agricultural areas.

children.name: Varacosa avara
children.description: A North American wolf spider formerly placed in Lycosa. Found in open fields and gardens from New England to the Gulf Coast.

children.name: Varacosa gosiuta
children.description: A western US wolf spider of the Great Basin and Colorado Plateau. Named after the Gosiute tribe. Found in arid grasslands and sagebrush.

children.name: Varacosa shenkeli
children.description: A southeastern US wolf spider found in pine forests and associated open habitats. Named for the arachnologist Ehrenfried Schenkel.

children.name: Wadicosa
children.description: A genus of medium-sized wolf spiders from Africa, southern Europe and Asia. They prefer damp habitats near water, where they are active hunters on the ground and among low vegetation.

children.name: Wadicosa fidelis
children.description: A widespread African wandering wolf spider found across the continent south of the Sahara. Active in damp grasslands and near water bodies.

children.name: Wadicosa benadiri
children.description: An East African Wadicosa found in Somalia and Kenya (named for the Benadir region). Lives in arid and semi-arid savanna habitats.

children.name: Wadicosa occidentalis
children.description: A West African wandering wolf spider found from Senegal to Cameroon. Prefers damp soils near rivers and lakes.
